title: Section 8 Application Filed After 11 Months of First Appearance Held Time-Barred: Calcutta High Court
description:
The Calcutta High Court, in *IDFC First Bank Ltd. and Anr. v. Shyamsundar Distributor and Anr.*, dismissed an appeal against the rejection of an application filed under Sections 5 and 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The Court held that the application was filed approximately 11 months after the appellant's first appearance in the suit, well beyond the prescribed period for submitting a written statement, thus constituting a waiver of the right to seek reference to arbitration.

The appellant, IDFC First Bank, was sued by the respondent following a dispute over a loan settlement. Despite appearing in the suit on April 10, 2024, and receiving three extensions for filing a written statement, the appellant never filed one. Instead, on March 6, 2025, after the suit was taken off the ex parte board at its instance, the appellant filed a Section 8 application to refer the dispute to arbitration, relying on a clause in the loan agreement. The trial court rejected this application, citing the 11-month delay beyond the Civil Procedure Code's period for filing a written statement, and also struck off the appellant's defence.

The High Court examined Section 8(1) of the Act, which mandates such applications to be made "not later than the date of submitting his first statement on the substance of the dispute." The Court distinguished *Booz Allen and Hamilton Inc. v. SBI Home Finance Ltd.*, noting that the present delay was not due to supplemental proceedings but the appellant's failure to act timely. It affirmed that the limitation for filing a Section 8 application is co-extensive with the period prescribed for filing a written statement (90 to 120 days), citing precedents from the Delhi, Bombay, and Karnataka High Courts. The Court found no exceptional circumstances, as recognized in *PIC Departmentals Pvt. Ltd. v. Sreeleathers Pvt. Ltd.*, to justify the delay.

Consequently, the appeal was dismissed, and the trial court's order, including the rejection of the Section 8 application and the striking off of the appellant’s defence for non-filing of the written statement, was affirmed.

title: Towards a Regulated End-of-Life Vehicle Regime: Decoding the Draft Motor Vehicles (RVSF) Amendment Rules, 2026
description:
India's vehicle scrapping framework is undergoing a significant overhaul with the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways' (MoRTH) draft Motor Vehicles (Registration and Functions of Vehicle Scrapping Facility) Amendment Rules, 2026. These proposed amendments, open for public feedback for thirty days, aim to enhance operational standards, digital integration, and enforcement mechanisms for Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facilities (RVSFs) by modifying the 2021 rules.

Key changes include a broader definition of "End-of-Life Vehicles" (ELVs) to encompass all unregistered, court-cancelled, or owner-declared waste vehicles, ensuring a wider regulatory net. The draft formalizes digital governance by defining a centralized "Portal" (Vscrap), integrated with the VAHAN database. A new Rule 8A mandates that vehicle registration cancellation due to destruction requires an electronically verified Certificate of Vehicle Scrapping (Form 4) from an RVSF, with exemptions for natural calamities or unforeseen events.

Crucially, Rule 13 is revamped to mandate precise, category-wise equipment specifications detailed in a new Table A, replacing generic requirements. This includes specific depollution systems, recovery units, tools, baling presses, radiation detection, and effluent treatment plants. Existing RVSFs have one year to comply. The audit and compliance framework under Rule 14 is fortified, requiring assessment of Table A equipment via a new Form 5. Non-compliance, such as failure to upload audit reports, can lead to registration suspension or cancellation.

These amendments signify a decisive move towards a robust, transparent, and environmentally compliant vehicle scrapping regime. By mandating stringent equipment standards, digital integration, and fortified audit mechanisms, the rules aim to eliminate informal scrapping practices, aligning India's ELV management with environmental sustainability and circular economy principles, while offering pragmatic transition periods and specific exemptions.

title: Crystallised Liabilities and the IBC Moratorium: NCLT Bengaluru Bars EPFO Claims for Section 7Q Interest and Section 14B Damages Accrued During Insolvency
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Bengaluru, in Regional Provident Fund Commissioner – II v. Antal Infotech Pvt. Ltd. and Anr., dismissed an application by the Employees’ Provident Fund Organisation (EPFO). The Tribunal's decision clarifies that the statutory protection under Section 36(4)(a)(iii) of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) applies solely to liabilities that have crystallised before the insolvency commencement date, and reinforces the strict adherence to IBC timelines.

The Corporate Debtor had defaulted on provident fund contributions, leading to EPF Act proceedings for principal dues, interest (Section 7Q), and damages (Section 14B). After the Corporate Debtor entered Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) and later liquidation, EPFO lodged a claim. The Liquidator admitted the pre-insolvency principal dues but rejected claims for interest and damages computed for the moratorium and post-liquidation periods, deeming them inadmissible under the IBC.

The NCLT dismissed the EPFO's application on two primary grounds. Firstly, it held the application time-barred under Section 42 of the IBC, emphasizing the absence of provisions for condonation of delay and noting that subsequent communications did not constitute a fresh cause of action. Secondly, on merits, the Tribunal distinguished earlier NCLAT judgments, relying on EPFO v. Rachna Jhunjhunwala. This precedent dictates that liabilities, particularly penal ones like interest and damages under Sections 7Q and 14B, must have crystallised before the insolvency commencement date to be admissible. The NCLT found no evidence that the disputed interest and damages, calculated for the moratorium period, had crystallised pre-insolvency.

This ruling solidifies that while provident fund dues are statutorily protected, this protection is contingent upon the liability being crystallised prior to insolvency. It underscores that the IBC's moratorium acts as a robust shield against the accrual of fresh penal liabilities during the insolvency process, placing the burden of proving pre-insolvency crystallisation firmly on the claimant.

title: Right of Redemption Extinguished Upon Publication of Sale Notice: DRAT Chennai Restores Auction Purchaser’s Rights in SARFAESI Proceedings
description:
The Debt Recovery Appellate Tribunal (DRAT) Chennai, in Khaja Moideen Leather Company v. Authorised Officer, Indian Bank and Ors., delivered a significant ruling clarifying that a borrower's right of redemption is extinguished upon the publication of a sale notice under the SARFAESI Act. This decision aims to balance the rights of secured creditors, borrowers, and auction purchasers, ensuring the statutory trajectory of enforcement proceedings.

The case arose after Khaja Moideen Leather Company successfully bid in a secured asset auction conducted by Indian Bank on October 29, 2025, following a sale notice on October 4, 2025. Despite the appellant making full payment, the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) Madurai issued an interim order on November 7, 2025, restraining the Bank from issuing the sale certificate until December 31, 2025. This stay was conditional upon the original borrowers/guarantors (respondents 2-4) paying the outstanding amount in instalments, effectively allowing them to redeem the property after the auction had concluded.

Aggrieved by this indefinite stay that deprived it of the auctioned property, the successful bidder appealed to the DRAT. The DRAT, presided over by Justice G. Chandrasekharan, reiterated the well-settled principle that the right of redemption is lost once the sale notice is published. It held that the borrowers could not resurrect this right post-auction by merely tendering payments in instalments. The Tribunal found that the DRT's interim order, issued after the sale, improperly stalled the statutory culmination of the enforcement process. Consequently, the DRAT set aside the effect of the DRT's interim order and directed the DRT Madurai to immediately dispose of the interim application and expeditiously adjudicate the main securitisation application on merits, strictly in accordance with the law that redemption rights cease upon sale notice publication. This judgment underscores the need for judicial discipline in granting interim stays in SARFAESI matters and protects the interests of bona fide auction purchasers.

title: Dr. Ramesh v. State of Maharashtra & Anr. (2026 INSC 635): Supreme Court Reaffirms Strict Compliance with the PCPNDT Act
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in Dr. Ramesh v. State of Maharashtra & Anr. (2026), delivered a significant judgment reaffirming the critical importance of strict compliance with the Pre-Conception and Pre-Natal Diagnostic Techniques (PCPNDT) Act, 1994. The case stemmed from an inspection of Dr. Ramesh's sonography centre, which revealed alleged deficiencies and blank entries in the mandatory statutory records, specifically Form F.

The appellant challenged the prosecution on two grounds: the competence of the Civil Surgeon to initiate proceedings and whether Form F deficiencies were merely technical errors. The Court rejected both arguments, confirming the Civil Surgeon's authority. Crucially, it ruled that deficiencies in Form F are not mere technical lapses but serious violations. It emphasized that Form F serves as a vital monitoring mechanism against illegal sex determination and female foeticide, and its incomplete or inaccurate maintenance triggers the statutory presumption under Section 4(3) of the Act, which shifts the burden of proof to the accused.

The judgment underscored the PCPNDT Act's role as essential social welfare legislation aimed at combating deeply rooted patriarchal preferences and the declining child sex ratio. Citing previous judgments and demographic data, the Court highlighted the ongoing need for rigorous enforcement to protect the dignity and existence of the girl child. The appeal was dismissed, upholding the cognizance taken against the appellant, thereby reinforcing that strict adherence to record-keeping obligations is indispensable for the Act's effective implementation.

title: NCLT Kolkata Dismisses GST Consultant’s Fee Claim: Reasonability and Proportionality as the Touchstone for CIRP Cost Recognition
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Kolkata, in Prateek S Jain & Associates v. Pratim Bayal and Anr., dismissed a GST consultant's application seeking payment of ₹6,48,000 in outstanding professional fees incurred during a Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). The consultant had provided GST compliance services for the Corporate Debtor, Nandini Impex, from before the CIRP commenced until September 2024, and had received partial payments from the erstwhile Resolution Professional (RP).

The applicant argued that the services were essential for the Corporate Debtor's operations, were acknowledged by the erstwhile RP through official communications and partial payments, thereby constituting an implied contract and legitimate Insolvency Resolution Process Costs (CIRP costs) under Section 5(13) of the IBC, even without a formal appointment letter or CoC resolution. The successor RP, however, countered that the claimed fees were unapproved, excessive, and grossly disproportionate to the Corporate Debtor's negligible business activity during CIRP (which primarily involved Nil GST returns), and that there was no formal engagement under the IBC framework.

The Tribunal affirmed that professional services essential for the Corporate Debtor's business as a going concern could, in principle, qualify as CIRP costs under Section 5(13)(c). However, it critically emphasized the RP's statutory duty to exercise commercial prudence and ensure all CIRP expenses are legitimate, reasonable, and proportionate. Applying these criteria, the NCLT found that despite the services being essential for statutory compliance, the claimed fees (e.g., ₹2,500 per month per state for filing Nil GST returns) were exorbitant and did not satisfy the test of reasonability, especially given the minimal business activity.

This ruling reinforces that while professional services for statutory compliance during CIRP may be recognized as CIRP costs, their reasonability and proportionality are subject to strict judicial scrutiny. Informal engagements and partial acknowledgements do not override the requirement for fees to meet the reasonableness standards prescribed by the IBC framework and IBBI Regulations. The judgment underscores the Resolution Professional's obligation to ensure CIRP costs are justified and commensurate with the actual work and the corporate debtor's financial condition, preventing the statutory priority of CIRP costs from being used to sanction inflated claims.

title: Supreme Court Sets Aside Reinstatement and Back Wages: Onus of Proof in Cases of Unauthorised Absence and Employee-Furnished Address
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in M/s Rifilis Engineering Pvt. Ltd. v. Arjun Gupta (May 22, 2026), reinforced that an employee seeking reinstatement and back wages must provide cogent documentary evidence to support claims of justified absence and attempted rejoining. Critically, the Court held that an employer cannot be penalized for communicating with an employee at the address voluntarily furnished by the employee at the time of appointment, placing the onus on the employee to update their contact details.

The dispute arose when Arjun Gupta absented himself without intimation from M/s Rifilis Engineering Pvt. Ltd. in May 2012. The employer sent a warning notice to the permanent address Gupta had provided. Gupta later claimed his absence was due to his mother's illness (verbally informed) and that he was denied re-entry upon his return. Despite a lack of documentary evidence from Gupta, the Labour Court and Allahabad High Court had initially ruled in his favor, directing reinstatement and back wages, partly noting the employer sent the notice to Gupta's permanent address in Bihar, not his temporary residence in Gautam Budh Nagar.

The Supreme Court meticulously examined the record and set aside these judgments. It clarified that the employer was not at fault for dispatching communication to the only address available in their records, which was furnished by the employee himself. The obligation to inform the employer of any change in residence rested squarely on Gupta. Furthermore, the Court found no documentary evidence to substantiate Gupta's claims regarding his mother's illness, verbal intimation to a superior, or his alleged attempt to rejoin duty. His prolonged unauthorized absence, without any written communication, could not be justified by mere oral assertions.

Consequently, the Supreme Court dismissed the employee's claim for reinstatement and back wages. This judgment underscores the necessity of evidentiary rigor in labour adjudication, emphasizing the employee's responsibility to provide updated contact information and robust documentary proof for absence justifications and claims of illegal termination.

title: Death Does Not Discharge the Debt: Bombay High Court on Maintenance Decrees and the Limits of Spousal Obligations Post-Mortem
description:
The Bombay High Court, in a significant May 2026 ruling, authoritatively clarified that while a maintenance decree survives the husband's death and binds his estate, a divorced wife cannot seek enhancement of that maintenance from the deceased husband’s estate. This judgment arose from Family Court Appeal No. 74 of 2023, where Warsha Eleekusumchand Javeri, divorced in 1999 and awarded Rs. 6,000 monthly maintenance, sought arrears and an enhancement after her ex-husband XYZ died intestate in 2012. The Family Court granted arrears but denied enhancement, prompting Warsha's appeal.

The Division Bench formulated two precise legal questions: (1) whether a divorce maintenance decree can be enforced against a deceased husband's estate, and (2) whether a divorced wife may seek enhancement of permanent maintenance against the estate. The respondent conceded the first point, but robustly opposed enhancement, invoking the Latin maxim actio personalis moritur cum persona, arguing that a claim for enhanced maintenance is a fresh claim requiring a judicial determination involving living parties.

The Court unequivocally answered the first question affirmatively, holding that a finalised maintenance decree, like any civil decree, endures post-death and is executable against the husband’s estate. This aligns with Section 37(1) of the Special Marriage Act, 1954, aiming to ensure the wife's lifetime maintenance. However, on the core question of enhancement, the Court ruled negatively. It reasoned that Section 37, especially sub-section (2) concerning variation, presupposes the continued existence of both living parties for a judicial determination of changed circumstances. The right to seek enhancement is personal (in personem) and extinguishes upon the husband's death, as legal heirs bear no personal obligation to maintain the ex-wife, and a new liability cannot be created post-mortem.

Dismissing the appeal, the High Court established a clear distinction: existing maintenance decrees are binding on the estate, ensuring ongoing payments and arrears. However, the right to seek enhancement is personal and extinguished upon the husband's demise, fixing the estate's liability at the existing decree's quantum. This ruling underscores the importance of securing adequate maintenance during the husband's lifetime, as no enhancement can be sought from the legal heirs thereafter.

title: Supreme Court Recognises the Fundamental Right to Walk: A Transformative Step Towards Pedestrian Justice
description:
The Supreme Court, in Maniyar Iliyaz @ Shaik Riyaz & Anr. v. P. Ayyappan & Ors., has declared the right to walk on demarcated footpaths a fundamental right under Articles 19(1)(d), read with 19(1)(a), (b), (c), and Article 21 of the Constitution. This landmark judgment originated from a tragic motor accident where a five-year-old child died due to the absence of safe pedestrian infrastructure, prompting a broader constitutional discussion on pedestrian safety and dignity.

The Court emphasized that walking is the most basic form of human movement and must receive primary constitutional protection, linking it to dignity, safety, and access to public life. This recognition imposes a corresponding duty on urban development authorities, municipal corporations, municipalities, and panchayats to provide and maintain safe pedestrian infrastructure. The ruling shifts the legal focus from merely compensating victims post-accident to proactively preventing tragedies by ensuring adequate facilities.

The judgment clarifies that the Motor Vehicles Act is insufficient for comprehensive pedestrian protection, as it is primarily vehicle-centric. It calls for a dedicated statutory framework and a full-time regulator to enforce pedestrian rights, directing the judgment to concerned ministries and the Law Commission for review. Citizens are now entitled to seek restitutionary remedies against public authorities for violations of this right, independent of claims under the Motor Vehicles Act. Furthermore, the Court enhanced the compensation for the victim's family to ₹11,44,628.

This decision fundamentally challenges vehicle-centric urban planning by prioritizing pedestrians, strengthening the accountability of public authorities for unsafe roads and inadequate infrastructure. The Court has re-numbered the matter as an Article 32 petition, "Re: Fundamental Right to Walk and Footpath," ensuring ongoing consideration for the protection and enforcement of pedestrian rights.

title: Minor Victims Cannot Waive Justice: Rajasthan High Court Reaffirms POSCO Protections
description:
The Rajasthan High Court, in S.B. Criminal Miscellaneous (Petition) No. 3992/2022, decisively quashed a trial court's order that had closed proceedings in a gang rape and sexual assault case involving a minor victim under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act. The case originated from a 2021 FIR by a minor girl alleging gang rape, whose consistent Section 164 Cr.P.C. statement named the accused. However, the Investigating Officer filed a negative final report, which the Special POCSO Court accepted, closing the case solely based on the minor victim's subsequent "no objection" to pursue proceedings.

Challenging this closure, the petitioner argued that the investigation was flawed, the minor's expressed consent lacked legal validity, and the trial court failed its duty to scrutinize the allegations. Justice Anoop Kumar Dhand's analysis firmly established that a minor victim's consent (under 18) is legally immaterial for POCSO/rape offences, a principle that applies throughout all stages of proceedings. The Court deemed the trial court's act of summoning the minor directly inappropriate, stating that parents or guardians should have been involved. Minors lack the legal capacity and maturity to fully comprehend the consequences of withdrawing criminal proceedings, thus requiring stringent judicial scrutiny of such statements. Crucially, the petitioner's indefeasible right to legal assistance under Section 40 of the POCSO Act was also violated.

The High Court reinforced its position by relying on Supreme Court precedents, including State of Madhya Pradesh v. Madanlal, which underscored that compromise in rape cases is impermissible as such acts constitute crimes against dignity.

Consequently, the High Court set aside the trial court's order and remitted the matter. It directed the trial court to summon the minor's parents or guardian, afford them an opportunity to file a protest petition, and pass orders strictly in accordance with law based on evidence. Legal assistance via the District Legal Services Authority was mandated for the petitioner and her representatives if they lacked counsel. This judgment emphatically clarifies that POCSO protections for minor victims are absolute and cannot be circumvented by their expressions of disengagement, obligating courts to look beyond surface-level consent to uphold justice for child victims.

title: Omission of Section 13(8) Redemption Clause in Possession Notice Proves Fatal to Bank’s Recovery Action
description:
The Debt Recovery Appellate Tribunal (DRAT), Allahabad, in Indian Bank v. Gurudeo Kripa and Anr., has decisively reiterated the paramount importance of strict procedural adherence under the Securitization and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest (SARFAESI) Act, 2002. The Tribunal upheld the decision of the Debt Recovery Tribunal below, which had set aside Indian Bank's possession notice and all subsequent auction proceedings due to a critical omission: the mandatory redemption clause under Section 13(8) of the SARFAESI Act was absent from the Section 13(4) possession notice.

Indian Bank had initiated recovery actions against M/s. Gurudeo Kripa for a non-performing asset. Following the borrower's default, the bank issued a Section 13(4) possession notice which, by its own admission, inadvertently omitted the redemption clause as required by Appendix IV of the Security Interest (Enforcement) Rules, 2002. The bank contended that the redemption right was mentioned in the initial Section 13(2) demand notice, the borrower had ample opportunity to redeem (including via the Patna High Court), and the omission was a minor, typographical error.

However, the DRAT rejected these arguments. It emphatically held that the omission of the Section 13(8) redemption clause constitutes a fundamental defect, not a mere curable irregularity, that vitiates the entire enforcement chain. The Tribunal underscored that every provision of the SARFAESI Act and its Rules must be strictly complied with by secured creditors. It clarified that borrowers' statutory right to redeem the secured asset cannot be curtailed or diluted by their subsequent conduct or through opportunities granted in unrelated court proceedings. Consequently, the DRAT ruled that a defective possession notice renders all subsequent actions, including orders under Section 14, automatically invalid.

This judgment serves as a crucial reminder for banking institutions and legal practitioners that the SARFAESI Act, while a powerful recovery tool, demands meticulous adherence to prescribed forms and procedures. Failure to comply with mandatory statutory requirements, even due to inadvertence, can render the entire recovery mechanism unsustainable on procedural grounds.

title: Delhi High Court Upholds Finding of Wrongful Termination, Declines Interference Under Section 34
description:
The Delhi High Court dismissed cross-petitions filed by Telecommunications Consultants India Ltd. (TCIL) and Frans Global Infotech Ltd. (Frans) under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act. The court upheld an arbitral award concerning a State Wide Area Network project for the Government of Tripura, which TCIL had subcontracted to Frans.

The arbitral dispute stemmed from TCIL's termination of a Purchase Order with Frans. The Arbitral Tribunal found TCIL's termination wrongful, primarily because it failed to provide the contractually mandated 45-day notice period. Consequently, the Tribunal ruled the encashment of Frans's Performance Bank Guarantee and its blacklisting as unlawful, and rejected TCIL’s counter-claims.

TCIL challenged the finding of wrongful termination, citing Frans's alleged defaults and the doctrine of anticipatory breach. It also presented Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) communications as evidence. The High Court declined to interfere, affirming the Tribunal's correct interpretation that a 45-day notice was a condition precedent for valid termination. The court also noted TCIL's inconsistent contractual interpretation (doctrine of approbate and reprobate) and found no evidence of anticipatory breach by Frans, who had expressed willingness to perform. OEM communications were deemed insufficient by the Tribunal, a valid assessment of probative weight.

Frans, in turn, challenged the rejection of its claims for supplied items, expected profits, unpaid invoices, and reputational damage. The High Court upheld these rejections. It agreed that Frans failed to provide cogent evidence of actual loss for supplied items, expected profits, or reputational damage. For unpaid invoices, the court affirmed the Tribunal's finding that a back-to-back payment mechanism applied, and there was no evidence TCIL had received payment from the end client.

Reiterating the limited scope of interference under Section 34, the Court emphasized that arbitral findings are to be respected unless they are perverse, based on no evidence, or in conflict with the fundamental policy of Indian law. Finding no such grounds, both petitions were dismissed.

title: Outsourced Workers Cannot Claim Promotion or Regularisation Based on Long Service: Uttarakhand High Court
description:
The Uttarakhand High Court, in a significant ruling by Justice Manoj Kumar Tiwari, has clarified that outsourced or contract employees cannot claim promotion or regularisation based solely on long service or unauthorized designation upgrades. The decision stemmed from a writ petition filed by two outsourced stenographers challenging the reversal of their upgraded designation.

The petitioners, engaged through an outsourcing agency (UPNL) for Uttarakhand Jal Sansthan since 2012 and 2014, had their designation unilaterally changed from Stenographer to Senior Personal Assistant by a Chief General Manager in 2021. However, in April 2026, the competent authority restored their original designation, prompting the petitioners to challenge this reversion, arguing for continuity in the upgraded role and citing a government order.

The Court dismissed the petition, asserting that no direct employer-employee relationship exists between outsourced workers and the State Government. It ruled that long years of service do not automatically confer the status of regular State employees. Justice Tiwari highlighted that the 2021 designation upgrade was made without legal authority and was void ab initio, justifying the competent authority's decision to correct the error. The Court also clarified that outsourced employees have no inherent right to claim promotion.

This judgment significantly impacts public sector employment, reaffirming that administrative errors do not create enforceable rights. It underscores that the terms of engagement for outsourced workers are defined by their relationship with the outsourcing agency, not the government body utilizing their services, thus preventing an erroneous designation from forming a basis for future regularisation claims.

title: Mere Change in Project Name Cannot Defeat Statutory Rights of Home Buyers
description:
The Maharashtra Real Estate Appellate Tribunal has unequivocally held that a real estate project's identity is rooted in its underlying land and sanctioned permissions, not its marketing name. This pivotal ruling, in Shakti Anand v. Sahyog Homes Ltd. (June 9, 2026), prevents promoters from evading RERA obligations through superficial nomenclature changes.

The case involved Sahyog Homes Ltd. marketing flats as "BBJ Roma" on specific Mumbai land from 2006-2008, taking substantial payments from homebuyers (appellants). Subsequently, the promoters registered the same land with MahaRERA under the name "Verona." When appellants sought refunds for non-possession, MahaRERA dismissed their complaints, asserting they were not "Verona" allottees and "BBJ Roma" was unregistered.

The Tribunal overturned MahaRERA's decision, emphasizing that advertisements, "BBJ Roma" allotment letters, commencement certificates, and the "Verona" RERA registration all pertained to identical land parcels (CTS numbers). It concluded that project registration is tied to the immutable land and valid permissions, making the appellants legitimate allottees of the registered "Verona" project despite the name change.

Furthermore, the Tribunal affirmed that the allotment letters constituted valid, enforceable contracts under the RERA Act, which, as per Supreme Court precedent, applies retroactively to ongoing projects without completion certificates. Noting the promoters' decade-long delay in possession and violations of MOFA and RERA regarding agreement execution, the Tribunal directed a refund of paid amounts with interest (SBI Highest MCLR + 2%) and imposed costs. This judgment serves as a significant precedent, reinforcing RERA's protective framework for homebuyers against promoters' deceptive practices.

title: Banks as Custodians, Not Arbiters: Karnataka High Court Reaffirms Proportionality in Account Freezing Orders
description:
In a landmark judgment (Sri. Madhu v. IndusInd Bank Ltd. & Anr., 9th June 2026), the Karnataka High Court definitively ruled that banking institutions, when acting on freezing directions from investigating agencies, must strictly adhere to the specified scope and quantum of those directions. The Court clarified that a bank cannot unilaterally extend a freeze to an entire account based merely on the apprehension of receiving further directives in the future, thus circumscribing the limits of a bank's authority in such scenarios.

The case arose from IndusInd Bank freezing the petitioner Sri. Madhu's entire account, despite receiving specific requests from two different police stations totaling Rs. 25,000/-. The bank justified its full account freeze by citing the possibility of additional freezing requests from other agencies. Aggrieved by this disproportionate action, Sri. Madhu petitioned the High Court, challenging the bank's arbitrary exercise of power and seeking to unfreeze amounts exceeding the directed sum.

Justice Suraj Govindaraj's ruling underscored that a bank's power in implementing a freeze is derivative and ministerial, not independent. A bank functions as a custodian, obligated to implement only what is lawfully directed, and its authority is co-extensive with the terms of the received directions. The Court emphatically rejected the bank's apprehension of future directives as a valid legal basis, stating that administrative convenience and anticipatory action cannot substitute a subsisting legal mandate. Crucially, the judgment invoked the principle of proportionality, asserting that any restriction on a bank account must precisely correspond to the extent of the direction received.

Consequently, the High Court allowed the petition, directing the debit freeze to be restricted solely to the aggregate amount of Rs. 25,000/-, permitting the petitioner to operate the remaining balance and future credits. The Court further issued a prospective direction to IndusInd Bank, stipulating that in all future cases, it must strictly adhere to the tenor of directions received, earmarking only the specified sum unless a complete freeze is explicitly mandated or statutorily required. This judgment powerfully reaffirms that banks occupy a strictly ministerial role, upholding legal rights over administrative convenience.

title: Mandatory Use of Corrosion-Resistant Knives and Blades in Food Businesses: An Analysis of the FSSAI Directive dated 15th June 2026
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) issued a critical advisory on June 15, 2026, mandating Food Business Operators (FBOs) to use only food-grade, corrosion-resistant knives and cutting equipment. This directive, rooted in Schedule 4 of the Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011, addresses prevalent non-compliance, where rusted, corroded, or damaged equipment poses significant public health risks.

The FSSAI highlighted that substandard cutting tools can lead to physical (metal fragments), chemical (leaching toxins), and microbiological (pathogen growth on pitted surfaces) contamination of food. To counter this, FBOs are now explicitly required to: utilize only food-grade and corrosion-resistant equipment; maintain all tools in sound, hygienic condition free from defects; immediately remove and replace any unsuitable equipment; and implement rigorous cleaning and sanitisation protocols.

Non-compliance with this advisory will trigger actions under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006. State and Union Territory Commissioners of Food Safety, alongside FSSAI Regional Directors, are directed to ensure stringent enforcement through vigilant inspections. This directive underscores FSSAI's commitment to safeguarding public health by ensuring comprehensive food safety and hygiene across the industry.

title: Public Transport and the Sexual Harassment Act: The Bombay High Court Clarifies the Scope of “Workplace”
description:
The Bombay High Court, in a significant ruling on June 16, 2026, in Siddhesh Pradeep Satpute v. State Bank of India & Ors., clarified the scope of "workplace" under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 (POSH Act). The court quashed the findings of an Internal Complaint Committee (ICC) of the State Bank of India, holding that it lacked jurisdiction as the alleged incident did not occur at a "workplace" as defined by the Act.

The case involved an SBI employee accused of sexual harassment in a shared autorickshaw while commuting. The bank's ICC found him guilty. However, the High Court emphasized that the ICC's jurisdiction is strictly tied to the statutory definition of "workplace." Section 2(o)(v) of the POSH Act expands the definition of workplace to include transportation only when provided by the employer. Since the shared autorickshaw was public transport and not provided by either the petitioner's or the complainant's employer, the incident did not fall within the Act's definition of "workplace."

The Court underscored that jurisdiction is a threshold question, meaning the ICC must first establish that an incident occurred at a "workplace" before proceeding to inquire into the complaint's merits. By allowing the writ petition and setting aside the ICC's order, the judgment reinforces that ICCs must operate within the strict boundaries of their statutory authority. The Court explicitly stated it did not adjudicate the merits of the sexual harassment allegation, only the jurisdictional aspect. This ruling provides crucial guidance for employers and practitioners regarding the territorial limits of ICCs under the POSH Act.

title: The Sole Surviving Widow’s Right to Full Family Pension: A Clarification by the Punjab and Haryana High Court
description:
In a pivotal judgment delivered on May 22, 2026, the Punjab and Haryana High Court, in Manjit Kaur v. State of Punjab & Others, conclusively held that a sole surviving widow of a deceased government employee is entitled to 100% family pension, irrespective of her status as the second wife. This ruling resolves a longstanding interpretive controversy surrounding Notes 1 and 2 appended to Rule 6.17(4) of the Punjab Civil Services Rules, Volume II, and reaffirms that the State cannot misappropriate any portion of the family pension due to the legal heirs.

The petitioner, Smt. Manjit Kaur, was the second wife of Sh. Parshotam Lal Puri, a retired District Treasury Officer. His first wife had predeceased him in 1980, and a Legal Heir Certificate confirmed Smt. Manjit Kaur as the sole eligible claimant with no eligible minor children from the first marriage. Despite this, the Accountant General restricted her family pension to 50%, citing her status as the ‘second wife’ and invoking Notes 1 and 2 of the relevant rules.

Justice Namit Kumar meticulously examined these rules, finding them inapplicable. Note 1 applies only when an employee is survived by more than one widow simultaneously, a condition absent here as the first wife died decades earlier. Note 2's operation is premised on the existence of an eligible minor child from a pre-deceased wife, which was also not the case. The Court emphasized that apportionment rules are designed to distribute pension among multiple simultaneous claimants, not to reduce the share of a sole eligible heir. Relying on precedents like Harpal Kaur and Ram Dulari, the Court affirmed that the State cannot retain any portion of the family pension.

Consequently, the High Court allowed the writ petition, setting aside the impugned memo and declaring Smt. Manjit Kaur entitled to 100% family pension from November 14, 2011, along with arrears and 6% interest. This significant judgment clarifies that pension apportionment rules cannot be misapplied to penalize a sole surviving widow, even if a second wife, when the conditions for multiple claimants are not met, ensuring the full benefit reaches the deceased employee's family.

title: NCLAT Upholds CIRP Against Rana Kapoor-Linked Companies: Debt, Default and the Limits of Arbitration Challenges under the IBC
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) reinforced the creditor-friendly framework of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) in `Bindu Kapoor v. Sapan Mohan Garg (IRP) & Ors.`. It dismissed appeals by Bindu Kapoor, upholding the admission of Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) against Bliss Abode Pvt. Ltd. and Bliss House Pvt. Ltd., companies linked to Rana Kapoor.

The dispute stemmed from loan facilities by Indiabulls Housing Finance to the companies, personally guaranteed by Rana and Bindu Kapoor. Rana Kapoor's arrest in March 2020 triggered "material adverse effect" clauses in the agreements, leading to loan recall notices. Failure to repay resulted in arbitration proceedings, culminating in awards favouring the lender in February 2023. Subsequently, the debt was assigned to JC Flowers Asset Reconstruction, which initiated Section 7 IBC proceedings, which the NCLT admitted and NCLAT upheld.

The NCLAT rejected the appellant's challenges. It affirmed the loan recall's validity, finding the "material adverse effect" clause properly invoked due to Rana Kapoor's situation genuinely impacting repayment capacity. The Tribunal clarified that pending challenges to arbitral awards before the Delhi High Court did not preclude insolvency proceedings; the primary default was non-compliance with the 2020 recall notices, with arbitral awards serving only as additional evidence of debt and default.

Additionally, NCLAT distinguished IBC proceedings from mere execution actions, noting it wasn't a disguised recovery attempt given the prolonged non-repayment. It reiterated the limited scope of Section 7—to ascertain the existence of financial debt and default, not equitable considerations—relying on Supreme Court precedents. The NCLAT also confirmed that an arbitral award creates a fresh cause of action, rendering the Section 7 applications timely. This judgment significantly reinforces contractual default provisions and the IBC's effectiveness as a resolution mechanism, irrespective of parallel recovery disputes.

title: Vandana Garg (RP) v. Mysore Petro Chemicals: Scope of Section 14 Moratorium and the Survival of Underlying Real Estate Claims in CIRP
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) in Vandana Garg (RP) v. Mysore Petro Chemicals clarified the absolute nature of the Section 14 moratorium under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC), particularly regarding the creation of new liabilities. The case involved Mysore Petro Chemicals, an allottee, who sought to admit a claim in the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) of Raghuleela Builders (Corporate Debtor).

Mysore Petro Chemicals had invested ₹12.93 crore for a commercial unit in 2015. When the Corporate Debtor failed to deliver, Mysore Petro Chemicals appealed to the Maharashtra Real Estate Regulatory Appellate Authority (RERAA) in December 2020. Critically, the Corporate Debtor was admitted into CIRP on October 4, 2021, triggering the moratorium. Despite this, RERAA passed an ex-parte order in June 2022, directing the Corporate Debtor to pay compensation. The Resolution Professional (RP) rejected the claim based on this post-moratorium RERAA order, citing Section 14 and belated submission. The NCLT, however, admitted the claim.

The NCLAT, overturning the NCLT's decision, held that the RERAA order, having been passed during the subsistence of the moratorium (October 2021 - June 2022) and creating a new compensation liability, was ineffective against the Corporate Debtor. Relying on P. Mohanraj, the Tribunal emphasized that Section 14(1)(a) prohibits the continuation of proceedings that create fresh liabilities against the Corporate Debtor, distinguishing it from ABG Shipyard which allows adjudication of pre-existing liabilities in certain contexts. The issue of belated claim submission was deemed academic since the foundational RERAA order itself was void against the Corporate Debtor.

However, the NCLAT adopted an equitable approach, acknowledging that the IBC is not expropriatory. While the claim based on the RERAA order was not maintainable, the Tribunal recognized Mysore Petro Chemicals' underlying bona fide investment. It directed the RP to verify the ₹12.93 crore payment from the Corporate Debtor's records and include it as a claim. This judgment underscores the stringent scope of the Section 14 moratorium against new liabilities while reinforcing the RP's duty to identify and recognize genuine underlying claims, even in the absence of strict formal compliance with claim timelines.

title: Mandatory Compliance with Rule 9 of SARFAESI Rules: Supreme Court Quashes Sixteen-Year-Old Auction Sale for Statutory Non-Compliance
description:
In a landmark judgment, the Supreme Court of India in M.R. Vasumathi v. Authorized Officer and Ors. reaffirmed the mandatory nature of procedural safeguards under the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (SARFAESI Act). The Court quashed a sixteen-year-old auction sale of a mortgaged property due to non-compliance with Rule 9 of the Security Interest (Enforcement) Rules, 2002, despite concurrent findings by lower tribunals and the Madras High Court.

The case originated from a 1984 loan default, leading to a preliminary decree in 1997. Following the guarantor's death, Indian Bank invoked SARFAESI in 2009. The auction in 2010 saw the successful bidder pay 25% immediately, but the remaining 75% was paid beyond the stipulated 15-day period, and crucially, without a written agreement for extension as mandated by Rule 9(4). The legal heirs challenged this, but their petitions were dismissed by the DRT, DRAT, and High Court, citing delay and borrower indolence.

The Supreme Court emphasized that auction validity hinges strictly on statutory compliance, not equitable considerations. It ruled that Rule 9(3) (immediate 25% deposit) and Rule 9(4) (balance within 15 days or written extension) are mandatory, not merely directory. The Court found the balance payment on March 31, 2010, was ex facie beyond the March 26, 2010 deadline and lacked any written agreement for extension, rendering the sale invalid. It held that statutory non-compliance cannot be waived or regularized by the secured creditor.

Invoking its equitable jurisdiction under Article 142, the Supreme Court quashed the auction sale. It directed a full refund of the deposited amount to the auction purchaser with 7% interest. Concurrently, it granted the appellant, the legal heir, a one-time opportunity to redeem the mortgage by paying the original outstanding amount plus 5% interest. This judgment underscores strict adherence to SARFAESI procedural mandates, while balancing equities through Article 142 to ensure justice and protect parties' interests.

title: Scope and Limits of Section 11 Adjudication: The Delhi High Court on Prima Facie Arbitration Agreements, Waiver, and Parallel Proceedings
description:
The Delhi High Court, in VE Commercial Vehicles Limited v. Singh Enterprises (30th May 2026), delivered a significant ruling clarifying the scope of Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The judgment addressed complex questions concerning arbitration jurisdiction, seat determination, and the interplay between civil litigation and IBC proceedings arising from a terminated commercial dealership agreement.

The dispute commenced when VE Commercial Vehicles (petitioner) invoked arbitration after terminating a dealership agreement with Singh Enterprises (respondent) due to alleged non-compliance and outstanding payments. Singh Enterprises vigorously opposed the arbitration petition, contending the agreement was unexecuted, alleging forgery of signatures to alter contract terms (leading to a criminal complaint), and claiming the petitioner had waived its arbitration rights by previously filing two civil suits. Respondents also challenged the Delhi High Court's territorial jurisdiction, asserting exclusive jurisdiction of Indore courts, and highlighted a subsequent Section 8 IBC notice.

The Court meticulously analyzed Clause 34 of the Dealership Agreement, which stipulated Delhi as the "place of arbitration" and made Indore's exclusive jurisdiction "subject to" this arbitration clause. Relying on Supreme Court precedents, the Court unequivocally held that Delhi was the juridical seat, emphasizing party autonomy in seat selection. Regarding the validity of the arbitration agreement and forgery allegations, the Court ruled that at the Section 11 stage, its jurisdiction was limited to examining the prima facie existence of an arbitration agreement, not adjudicating disputed facts like fraud or forgery. It noted that the respondents themselves had invoked the arbitration clause, and the alleged forgery concerned only the term duration, not the arbitration agreement itself.

The Court rejected the waiver argument, finding that a withdrawn civil suit (with liberty to institute fresh proceedings) and another suit concerning a distinct cause of action did not constitute an irrevocable waiver of arbitration rights. Similarly, the IBC notice, issued after the arbitration invocation and not followed by formal insolvency proceedings, was deemed a "counter-blast" against the petitioner's legitimate claims. Consequently, the Court exercised its power under Section 11 to appoint Justice (Retd.) Kurian Joseph as the Presiding Arbitrator, preserving all substantive contentions for determination by the arbitral tribunal.

This judgment robustly affirms arbitration as a preferred dispute resolution mechanism, upholding party autonomy in seat selection, and maintaining the limited scope of judicial intervention at the Section 11 stage. It provides crucial clarity on resolving seat-venue conflicts, the treatment of IBC notices issued after arbitration invocation, and the impact of withdrawn or distinct civil suits on arbitration rights.

title: The Inherent Boundaries of Justice: Section 528 BNSS and the Limits of Pre-Trial Quashing in Cheque Dishonour Cases
description:
The Punjab and Haryana High Court, in Atul Vig v. Hunny Singh Ahluwalia & Anr., provided a crucial exposition on the interplay between the High Court's inherent jurisdiction under Section 528 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS), and the statutory framework for cheque dishonour cases under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. The judgment firmly reiterates that pre-trial quashing of a criminal complaint is an exceptional remedy, not a substitute for a full trial.

The case involved a complainant alleging that the petitioner dishonoured two cheques totaling Rs. 49 lakhs, issued against a friendly loan. The petitioner sought quashing, arguing the loan's undocumented cash nature, its fiscal impermissibility, and inconsistencies in the loan agreement. Crucially, the petitioner contended the cheques were forcibly extracted through coercion linked to a failed property transaction, supported by a prior police complaint alleging duress.

The High Court, drawing upon the principles governing inherent jurisdiction, emphasized its sparing and cautious exercise. It noted that the petitioner did not deny signing or issuing the cheques. This non-denial automatically triggered the statutory presumptions under Sections 118 and 139 of the NI Act, which posit that the cheque was issued in discharge of a legally enforceable debt. Rebutting these presumptions, including the coercion plea, constitutes disputed questions of fact that require the adduction of evidence during a trial. The court also observed that the police complaint alleging coercion lacked material demonstrating any further pursuit or action taken upon it.

Finding no merit in the petition, the High Court dismissed it. This decision underscores that the remedy of quashing is not intended to insulate an accused from trial where disputed questions of fact are involved. The non-denial of signatures on a dishonoured cheque, coupled with the operation of statutory presumptions, creates a strong prima facie case that necessitates adjudication through the trial process, thereby preserving the integrity of the Section 138 mechanism as a commercial remedy.

title: The Limitation Clock Starts with Inquiry, Not Ignorance: Supreme Court on Probate Revocation
description:
The Supreme Court, in Dhiraj Dutta v. Anirban Sen & Ors., ruled that the limitation period for probate revocation begins with constructive notice, not merely actual knowledge. This significant decision clarified the interplay between Section 263 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925, and Article 137 of the Limitation Act, 1963, reaffirming that the law aids the vigilant.

The case involved an application filed in 2022 to revoke a probate granted in 1995, nearly three decades earlier. The respondents, who were nephews-in-law of the testatrix, claimed they only discovered the probate's existence in 2019. However, the appellant had initiated mutation proceedings in 2010-11, and notices concerning these proceedings were served to the respondents' predecessors-in-interest in 2013.

The Court determined that while Section 263 allows revocation for "just cause," it lacks a specific limitation period, thus invoking Article 137 of the Limitation Act, which prescribes a three-year period from when the "right to apply accrues." Crucially, the Supreme Court held that the notices served during the 2013 mutation proceedings constituted constructive notice of the appellant's probated claim. A "reasonably prudent person" receiving such notices affecting their property rights would have diligently inquired into the basis of these proceedings, which would have revealed the probate.

By choosing to ignore these notices, the respondents failed to exercise due diligence. Consequently, the Court concluded that the limitation period commenced in 2013, making their 2022 revocation application hopelessly time-barred. This judgment reinforces that parties cannot indefinitely postpone legal challenges by claiming ignorance when circumstances reasonably require inquiry, thereby strengthening the finality and certainty attached to probate grants.

title: Unilateral Appointment of Arbitrators Under Section 12(5) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996: A Critical Analysis of Air Force Naval Housing Board v. M/s N G Constructions
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Air Force Naval Housing Board v. M/s N G Constructions (2026), delivered a landmark ruling on the unilateral appointment of arbitrators under Section 12(5) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The case arose from a construction contract where the Air Force Naval Housing Board unilaterally appointed a sole arbitrator, as per a contractual clause. Subsequently, the Board itself sought to set aside the arbitral award, arguing that its own appointment violated Section 12(5) read with the Seventh Schedule of the Act, which mandates automatic ineligibility for party-employees or their nominees.

Justice Avneesh Jhingan, citing Supreme Court decisions like Bhadra International and the Delhi High Court's Mahavir Prasad Gupta, affirmed that Section 12(5) renders such unilateral appointments void ab initio. These precedents establish that the ineligibility stems from operation of law, ensuring equal treatment of parties. Crucially, the Court reiterated that the proviso to Section 12(5) demands an "express agreement in writing" for waiver, which cannot be inferred from a party's participation in proceedings or conduct. Such conduct is inconsequential and cannot cure the statutory defect. Furthermore, the judgment upheld that even the party making the unilateral appointment retains the right to challenge the award, as the tribunal's inherent lack of jurisdiction renders the proceedings a nullity.

Consequently, the Court allowed the petition, setting aside the impugned award dated 27th July 2022. This ruling robustly reinforces the legislative intent of the 2015 Amendment, ensuring impartiality and integrity in the arbitral process. It clarifies that fundamental jurisdictional defects arising from statutory ineligibility cannot be overcome by implied consent or acquiescence, serving as a critical precedent, especially for unilateral appointment clauses prevalent in government contracts.

title: Jayakrishna Menon v. Krishnankutty & Ors.
description:
The Supreme Court, in Jayakrishna Menon v. Krishnankutty & Ors. (June 9, 2026), issued a stern ruling on contempt of court and animal welfare. The case stemmed from a custody dispute over Raman, Kerala's tallest captive elephant, and focused on the alleged wilful disobedience of a solemn undertaking given to the Court.

The petitioner, Jayakrishna Menon, claimed Raman belonged to the Mata Amritanandamayi Mutt and had been entrusted to Respondent No. 1, Krishnankutty, solely for care. Krishnankutty, however, asserted ownership, leading to criminal proceedings and a protracted legal battle. During pending appeals before the Supreme Court, Krishnankutty's counsel explicitly undertook on August 6, 2025, that Raman would not be used for any commercial or temple activities in the interim.

Despite this undertaking, the petitioner alleged Raman's continued deployment in temple festivals and public events. Respondent No. 1 initially denied the allegations but later admitted in written submissions that Raman participated in a "brief ceremonial ritual" at a temple, attributing it to veterinary advice for walks and another elephant's illness. A State inspection report subsequently corroborated Raman's presence at a temple festival, confirming the breach.

The Court found the violation of the undertaking "beyond dispute," established by Respondent No. 1's admission and the State's report. Expressing profound concern over the commercial exploitation of Raman in defiance of its order, the Court emphasized its duty towards voiceless beings, stating it could not be a "mute spectator" to such conduct. State authorities, however, were discharged, as their attempts to inspect were deemed genuine despite initial challenges.

Consequently, the Supreme Court held Respondent No. 1 guilty of contempt of court for wilful disobedience and imposed a fine of INR 2,000. Crucially, prioritizing Raman's health and welfare pending final adjudication, the Court directed the State of Kerala to take temporary custody of the elephant, house him in an appropriate rescue or rehabilitation centre, and care for him at its expense. This judgment powerfully reaffirms the sanctity of judicial undertakings and highlights the judiciary's proactive role in safeguarding animal welfare.

title: Karnataka High Court Directs Enforcement of K-RERA Recovery Certificates Within Eight Weeks
description:
The Karnataka High Court, in Rajesh Rao C.V. and Others v. State of Karnataka and Others, has decisively reiterated that recovery certificates issued by the Karnataka Real Estate Regulatory Authority (K-RERA) must be enforced promptly. The judgment, delivered on 27 April 2026, addresses a critical practical challenge where successful claimants under the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA Act), face delays in actual recovery despite obtaining valid certificates.

The petitioners approached the High Court seeking a writ of mandamus, contending that designated authorities had failed to act upon K-RERA recovery certificates issued against Respondent No. 6. They emphasized that Section 40 of the RERA Act and Rule 25 of the Karnataka Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Rules, 2017, mandate the recovery of such amounts as arrears of land revenue. The core grievance was that the issuance of certificates had not led to actual enforcement.

The Court consistently observed that once a recovery certificate is issued under RERA, the concerned authorities are under an undeniable obligation to recover the specified amount. It clarified that enforcement is not optional or discretionary, especially given the appointment of a Special Deputy Commissioner to handle such cases. The Court concluded that the petitioners had a clear legal right to demand enforcement, and the authorities had a corresponding duty.

Consequently, the High Court allowed the writ petition, issuing a writ of mandamus. It directed the respondent authorities to execute the recovery certificates and recover the amounts within eight weeks from the order's receipt. Furthermore, it granted petitioners the liberty to provide details of the debtor's properties to assist the Special Deputy Commissioner in the recovery process.

This judgment holds significant importance as it reinforces the enforceability of RERA-issued recovery certificates, ensuring that administrative inaction does not undermine regulatory relief. It strengthens the RERA Act's implementation mechanism by making authorities accountable and translating adjudicatory orders into actual recovery for beneficiaries, affirming that a recovery certificate is an enforceable statutory instrument.

title: DNA Testing and Paternity Disputes: The Supreme Court Reaffirms the Balance of Interests
description:
In Chaturbhuj Pradhan v. Amar Pradhan & Anr. (2026), the Supreme Court of India upheld a lower court's direction for DNA testing to determine paternity. This ruling re-emphasizes the legal framework for court-ordered DNA tests in civil proceedings, founded on evidentiary insufficiency and the balance of competing interests.

The case centered on Amar Pradhan, born in 1999, seeking paternity declaration against Chaturbhuj Pradhan (CP), who consistently denied fatherhood. After years of litigation, Amar, upon majority, filed a civil suit. Both trial and High Courts directed a DNA test, prompting CP's appeal based on objections like compelled testing, absence of "eminent need," and res judicata from prior summary maintenance proceedings.

Reviewing precedents, the Court affirmed DNA tests are exceptional, not routine, warranted only when paternity is unequivocally in issue and no other evidence provides a conclusive resolution. It reinforced the "balance of interests" framework, weighing the alleged father's privacy against the child's fundamental right to lineage and identity.

Dismissing CP's appeal, the Court found his prolonged paternity denial left no alternative proof, making the DNA test indispensable. It rejected the res judicata argument, clarifying that findings from summary criminal maintenance proceedings lack finality for a civil paternity suit. The Court held Amar's compelling right to establish parentage decisively outweighed CP's privacy interest, directing the trial court to proceed with the DNA examination.

This judgment underscores that judicial power to order DNA tests is circumscribed, exercised only when evidentiary alternatives are exhausted and the balance of interests demands it. It reinforces that privacy is not absolute, yielding to significant competing rights, and clarifies the limited precedential value of summary legal findings.

title: Can Borrowers Avoid Pre-Deposit in SARFAESI Appeals? Calcutta High Court Settles the Debate
description:
The Calcutta High Court, in Jainco Projects (India) Ltd. v. UCO Bank and Ors., unequivocally reaffirmed the mandatory nature of the pre-deposit requirement under Section 18 of the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (SARFAESI Act) for appeals. The Court clarified that borrowers cannot circumvent this statutory obligation, even if the appeal arises from an interlocutory order concerning a One Time Settlement (OTS) proposal.

The petitioner, Jainco Projects, had their application to compel UCO Bank to implement an OTS scheme rejected by the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT). Challenging this, they appealed to the Debts Recovery Appellate Tribunal (DRAT) and sought a complete waiver of the Section 18 pre-deposit, arguing the DRT's order was merely procedural. The DRAT, however, directed a 50% deposit, which Jainco then contested before the Calcutta High Court under Article 227.

The High Court meticulously distinguished the case from Sunshine Builders, explaining that an order rejecting an OTS proposal directly impacts the borrower’s financial liability and the secured creditor’s recoverable dues, making it a substantive determination, not merely procedural. Orders affecting substantive rights, even if interlocutory, attract the mandatory pre-deposit under Section 18. The Court reiterated that this statutory provision creates an absolute bar against entertaining an appeal unless the prescribed deposit (50%, reducible to 25% by DRAT) is made, and neither the DRAT nor the High Court possesses the authority to waive it entirely.

Dismissing the petition, the High Court upheld the DRAT’s order. This judgment is crucial for clarifying the distinction between procedural and substantive orders for Section 18 purposes. It strengthens the principle that appellate remedies under the SARFAESI Act are conditional, not absolute, thereby discouraging frivolous litigation and ensuring the expeditious resolution of debt recovery proceedings. The ruling provides significant guidance for disputes involving OTS schemes and reinforces judicial adherence to the legislative objective of maintaining financial discipline and a structured appellate process.

title: SARFAESI Action Under Judicial Scrutiny: DRT Chandigarh Awards Compensation to Auction Purchasers While Setting Aside Illegal Sale
description:
The Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT), Chandigarh, in S.G.K. Industrial Corporation and Ors. v. Authorized Officer, Indian Bank and Ors., set aside an e-auction conducted by Indian Bank under the SARFAESI Act, emphasizing that the Act’s objective of expedited debt recovery does not supersede the mandatory procedural safeguards. The ruling highlights that expediency cannot justify arbitrary actions or overlook meticulous legislative requirements.

The Bank's enforcement actions were marred by significant procedural irregularities. Crucially, it violated the mandatory 15-day notice period by publishing the sale notice on March 17th for an auction scheduled on March 29th. Furthermore, the Bank sold two distinct mortgaged plots belonging to different owners as a single unit, disadvantaging the borrowers by potentially reducing buyer interest and value. Other lapses included ignoring a higher One Time Settlement (OTS) proposal of Rs. 42 lacs from the borrowers, choosing instead to sell the property for Rs. 33.52 lacs, and failing to properly serve notices to the legal heirs of a deceased guarantor.

The Tribunal found the Bank's conduct "clumsy" and the auction fundamentally flawed. It underscored that previous failed auction attempts do not excuse non-compliance with statutory provisions, particularly the 15-day notice period, citing precedents from the Supreme Court and the Punjab and Haryana High Court. While acknowledging the auction purchasers as bona fide, the DRT clarified that the principle protecting confirmed auctions (the Bafna principle) does not extend to sales vitiated by cumulative and fundamental procedural irregularities.

Consequently, the DRT set aside the illegal auction. It directed Indian Bank to refund the entire sale consideration, stamp duty, and registration charges to the auction purchasers, along with 12% interest per annum. Additionally, recognizing the inconvenience caused by the Bank's illegal acts, the Tribunal awarded Rs. 30 lacs in compensation to the auction purchasers. The Bank was granted liberty to conduct a fresh auction in strict compliance with all statutory requirements, reaffirming robust judicial oversight in SARFAESI proceedings.

title: Regulating Environmental Compliance Through Product-Specific Standards: A Critical Examination of the Proposed Ecomark Criteria for Paints, Batteries, Paper, and Allied Products
description:
India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has proposed significant amendments (G.S.R. 452(E), 2026) to the Ecomark Rules, 2024, aiming to bolster environmental standards for manufactured products. This initiative, part of the broader LiFE Mission, updates the 1991 Ecomark scheme and entrusts the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) with its implementation. The draft notification is open for a 60-day public consultation to ensure participatory governance.

The proposed amendments introduce stringent, product-specific criteria across six categories:

1. Paints, Varnishes & Powder Coatings: Mandates BIS compliance, environmental clearances, and QR codes for transparency. It sets strict limits on Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs), toxic heavy metals (e.g., mercury, lead), formaldehyde, and bans persistent organic pollutants. Manufacturers must use 25% renewable energy, comply with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), possess ISO 14001:2015 certification, and use recyclable packaging. 2. Batteries: Requires CPCB EPR portal registration and display. Limits mercury and cadmium, prohibits chlorine-based plastics in primary casings/packaging, and sets graduated mandates for domestically recycled lead (50-70%). Units must meet effluent/emission standards, reduce energy consumption by 20% (preferably renewable), and ensure proper waste disposal. 3. Paper & Paper Products: Emphasizes sustainable raw material sourcing (e.g., 60% non-wood, 80% wastepaper), elemental/total chlorine-free bleaching, and strict limits on absorbable organic halides. It prohibits carcinogenic biocides, bans PFAS/optical brightening agents in food-contact paper, mandates chemical recovery plants for zero black-liquor discharge, and requires online effluent/emission monitoring. Specific norms for freshwater and electricity consumption are also prescribed. 4. Wood & Wood Substitute Products: Demands 100% sustainably sourced wood (agro-forestry/certified), utilization of agricultural residues for biomass, and industrial by-products for gypsum boards. It promotes natural adhesives, limits non-biodegradable materials (banning plastic in furniture), restricts formaldehyde, heavy metals, and VOCs, and requires 100% waste material utilization. 5. Fire Extinguishers: Prohibits ozone-depleting substances, mandates Ecomark-certified paint, bans halogenated plastics, and requires over 90% recycled metal components. Products must list ingredients, comply with EPR, and use extinguishing agents free from heavy metals and fluorinated organic compounds. ISO 14001:2015 certification is also required. 6. Coir & Coir Products: Stipulates mechanical fibre extraction, traceable natural rubber, and strict limits on residual pesticides, heavy metals, and harmful dyes. It mandates 25-30% renewable energy use, rainwater harvesting, comprehensive effluent treatment for wet processing, 100% treated wastewater utilization, and cleaner fuels in operations. Packaging must be compostable/biodegradable with end-of-life recycling instructions.

These amendments collectively promote key themes: mandatory BIS and environmental clearances, digital transparency via QR codes, robust EPR compliance, ISO 14001 certification, renewable energy integration, stringent hazardous substance elimination, sustainable packaging, and circular economy principles. This comprehensive regulatory push is poised to significantly shape India's manufacturing environmental footprint, aligning it with national sustainability goals.
